text: The robotic instruments are positioned inside the pelvis, beginning the radical prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon has dropped the bladder, revealing the prostate gland within the pelvic cavity during the robotic prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon retracts the right vas deferens while isolating the external iliac vein, using robotic instruments within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to fully expose the pelvic anatomy, preparing for lymph node dissection at the conclusion of the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon displays the anatomy on the left side of the pelvis, including structures relevant to the robotic radical prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the external iliac vein, ensuring it is fully exposed in preparation for the upcoming lymph node dissection during the robotic radical prostatectomy.
The surgeon dissects the endopelvic fascia, carefully isolating the puboprostatic ligaments adjacent to the prostate.
The surgeon isolates and retracts the fat within the surgical plane to reveal the underlying prostate anatomy, ensuring clear visualization for further dissection.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to fulgurate the superficial dorsal venous complex, controlling bleeding in the area.
The surgeon identifies a vein responsible for draining blood from the prostate, which connects to the pelvic veins and ultimately returns blood to the heart.
The surgeon removes the periprostatic or anterior pelvic fat, enhancing the exposure of the prostate for further dissection.
The surgeon exposes the endopelvic fascia, revealing the connective tissue layer surrounding the prostate within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon incises the tissue surrounding the prostate to facilitate its dissection from the adjacent pelvic muscles.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to cut the puboprostatic ligament, separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies the puboprostatic ligament, a strong connective tissue attaching the prostate to the underside of the pubic bone, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes similar anatomy on the left side and proceeds to dissect the puboprostatic ligament on the left, using precise techniques to separate it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon isolates the dorsal venous complex and ligates it using a suture, ensuring secure closure of the vessel within the surgical field.

The surgeon visualizes a large vein responsible for draining blood from the prostate, which is a potential source of significant bleeding during open surgery. The robotic approach provides excellent visualization and control over this vein.
The surgeon begins the dissection between the bladder neck and the prostate, carefully separating these structures to progress with the prostatectomy.
The surgeon uses the catheter balloon to visually delineate the boundary between the prostate, located at the top of the screen, and the bladder, positioned at the bottom.
The surgeon dissects down to the catheter, which is positioned to delineate the boundary between the prostate and bladder.
The surgeon visualizes the catheter and uses it as a landmark to delineate the space between the prostate and the bladder neck, aiding in the precise dissection of these structures.
The surgeon uses the robotic fourth arm to bring the catheter into the surgical field and secures it, providing a clear visual landmark for further dissection between the prostate and bladder neck.
The assistant outside the patient pulls on the catheter, elevating it to enhance exposure of the surgical field, aiding in the dissection between the bladder neck and the prostate.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection, carefully separating the tissue between the back of the prostate and the bladder, with the prostate visible at the top of the screen and the bladder at the bottom.
The surgeon dissects down to the level of the vas deferens and seminal vesicles, revealing these structures in the surgical field as part of the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon secures the vas deferens using an absorbable clip, ensuring stability within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs dissection on the opposite side to elevate the seminal vesicles and vas deferens, located posterior to the prostate.
The surgeon clips and transects blood vessels supplying the prostate, then continues the posterior dissection by carefully separating the rectum from the prostate.
The surgeon incises the deep dorsal vein complex after completing the dissection down to the level of the vas deferens and seminal vesicles.
The surgeon maintains the suspension suture in place, visibly supporting the urethra or bladder tube against the inside of the pubic bone.
The surgeon performs nerve-sparing dissection of the neurovascular bundle on the right side, carefully separating it from the prostate tissue.
The surgeon carefully dissects the neurovascular bundle from the prostate, focusing on the nerves that insert along the prostate surface.
The surgeon transitions to dissecting the prostatic pedicle, focusing on separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon clips and cuts the blood vessels supplying the prostate, ensuring hemostasis during the procedure.
The surgeon approaches the neurovascular bundle during the nerve-sparing portion of the prostatectomy, preparing to carefully dissect it from the prostate.
The neurovascular bundle is completely separated from the prostate on the right side, with the surrounding tissue clearly dissected away.
After completing the dissection of the neurovascular bundles on both sides, the surgeon proceeds to dissect the urethra, focusing on the area where it connects to the prostate.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the urethra, aiming to preserve as much of it as possible while ensuring complete removal of prostate cancer tissue.
The surgeon focuses on the apex of the prostate, a region where positive margins are frequently observed, ensuring careful dissection to minimize the risk of leaving cancerous tissue.
The surgeon performs a delicate dissection around the urethra, aiming to preserve its structure while ensuring complete removal of cancerous tissue.
The surgeon performs a pelvic lymph node dissection on both sides after freeing the prostate, carefully avoiding the obturator nerve during the procedure.
The surgeon performs lymph node dissection, carefully removing lymphatic tissue to assess for cancer presence and to excise potential cancerous nodes.
The surgeon places the dissected lymph nodes, prostate, seminal vesicles, and vas deferens into a specimen bag for removal.
The surgeon applies a hemostatic agent along the neurovascular bundles to minimize bleeding, followed by performing an anastomosis to reconnect the bladder neck to the urethra.
The assistant manipulates the catheter externally to guide the placement of the needle internally, facilitating precise positioning during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the posterior plate of the anastomosis, visually confirming the connection between the bladder neck and urethra.
The surgeon brings the suture around to complete the anastomosis, visually connecting the bladder neck to the urethra.
The surgeon brings in the string for the specimen bag and places a drain in the surgical field.
